So they're all there. You've been Australia, you've been Hong Kong, you've been Thrillbread Racing, you've been hard-ass racing, you've been everything. That didn't used to exist. And so for a year, so I've lived here, I flew from here every Saturday to New York, and I went to Belmon or Acre Duct, whichever was in session, and I bet the Thrillbreads. And it's all I did. And I was okay at it. But I made over $100,000 during the year. And there were two things necessary to do that and a major discovery that came out of it. The two things that are necessary is loss limits and win limits. So in a given setting, say Saturday, as soon as I lost X amount of dollars, I left. And as soon as I won X amount of dollars, if it was the first race, I left. So you flew all the way from Cleveland to New York, you rented a car, you drove your ass all the way out to Belmon. And if the first race, my number was $3,000 at the time. If I'm up $3,000, the $3,000 is in my boot. And I'm on my way back to the airport. And if it's four hours before I can get a flight, I don't care, I'm away from the gambling. So those are two things. Plus, you have to know how to handle getting the races. But beyond that, win and loss limits. The reason nobody can ever win, for the most part, even in that environment, is because they'll say to themselves, well, I flew all the way here and I rented a car and I came all the way out to the track. And I really don't want to go home early anyway, so just hang around for one more race. I'll just bet a little and they bet it all back. So it's really about their behavior as much as it is anything. And so in the casinos, it's even more so. So they will stay. They will bet it all back. They will stay in a losing session because they ordered a drink. So 20 minutes from now, somebody's going to bring a drink, so they'll stay at a bad table forever and ever and ever and ever and ever. And so their expectation makes all that okay. Nobody goes to the track expecting to win. Nobody goes to the casino expecting to win. They've all been pre-programmed to expect to lose. And almost everybody goes with a budget of how much they're going to lose. Most people go to the racetrack, predetermined, okay, we're willing to lose $200 tonight, which virtually guarantees they're going to lose $200. And as Bob said, you could un-rig the games and they'd still probably manage to lose the money. It would just take longer. So there's a thermostat being set for them. There's a structure everybody buys into which has an expectation of losing money and it pretty much then governs their behavior and their outcomes. And if by accident somebody wins, generally speaking, they still get back to where the thermostat was set, which is a loss of how much because they'll modify their behavior. They'll double up, they'll parlay, they'll bet more, they'll do whatever in order to get back to the thermostat. So as an aside, as a marketing issue, we can often, if you think about it, pre-calibrate somebody to have a certain set of expectations that will then govern their behavior in their relationship with us. But what's important in this conversation is that we all have these thermostats. And generally speaking, we set them ourselves. And so think of how many times you do this. So most people have a sort of a whole set of superstitions, for example. So if you've ever had a couple of bad things happen right at the beginning of a day. So I don't know, let's say it's 8 o'clock in the morning and you realize your kid didn't come home all night and now the cruiser arrives and dumps him out. And you're sifting through something and you find a bill you thought had been paid three months ago and now they're going to disconnect the lights or something because your secretary, your spouse didn't send the payment. And you know, a couple of little irritants, let's say, like that. What people will often say, and that I have to catch myself and not say is, when it starts bad, it keeps going bad. If the day starts like this, it ain't going to get any better. It'd be better if you could just go back to bed, sleep until tomorrow and wait. Well, what are we doing? We're setting the thermostat, right? And so we're predetermining. So when you think about money, I believe most people are pre-calibrated.
